channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Activated Carbon Filtration
item.description:
Activated carbon removes volatile organic compounds (VOCs), odors, and some gases through adsorption, a process distinct from mechanical filtration. Carbon atoms on the surface attract and hold pollutant molecules until the carbon becomes saturated.

Adsorption Mechanism

Activated carbon has an extremely large surface area per unit mass—a single gram can have a surface area of 500–3,000 square meters depending on processing. VOC molecules are drawn to this surface by weak intermolecular forces (van der Waals forces). The pollutants adhere to the carbon but are not chemically combined; saturation occurs when binding sites are occupied.

item.title: AHAM Verifide Certification
item.description:
AHAM Verifide certification indicates that an air purifier has been tested by an independent laboratory and verified to deliver its advertised CADR performance. The program reduces the risk of inflated manufacturer claims.

Certification Process

The Association of Home Appliance Manufacturers (AHAM) administers a certification program where:

  1. Manufacturers submit their air purifier models for third-party laboratory testing.
  2. The laboratory tests the device using the ANSI/AHAM AC-1 standard in a 1,008 cubic foot test chamber.
  3. Three separate CADR values (smoke, dust, pollen) are measured and reported.
  4. If results pass, the manufacturer receives the AHAM Verifide seal and certificate.
  5. The certified unit and its CADR values are listed in the AHAM directory.

What It Means vs. Manufacturer Claims

  • Certified (AHAM Verifide): CADR ratings have been independently verified. The device displays an AHAM seal on packaging listing the three CADR numbers.
  • Uncertified (manufacturer-claimed): CADR values are based on in-house or contracted testing, without third-party verification. These claims may be inflated.

Studies show that some uncertified products claim significantly higher CADR values than similar certified products, suggesting manufacturer testing can overstate performance.

item.title: Air Changes Per Hour (ACH)
item.description:
ACH quantifies how effectively air is being recirculated or exchanged in an enclosed space. It connects mechanical ventilation rates or air purifier performance to the time required to meaningfully improve air quality throughout a room.

Definition and Calculation

ACH is calculated by dividing the total airflow rate (in cubic feet per minute) by the room’s volume (in cubic feet), then multiplying by 60 to convert to hourly rate:

ACH = (Airflow in CFM × 60) ÷ Room Volume (cubic feet)

item.title: ASHRAE Standards for Indoor Air Quality
item.description:
ASHRAE (American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers) publishes consensus standards for indoor air quality and ventilation. Multiple standards address residential air quality with different priorities and timelines.

ASHRAE 62.2-2016: Residential Ventilation Standard

ASHRAE 62.2 (“Ventilation and Acceptable Indoor Air Quality in Residential Buildings”) is the primary standard for residential IAQ and is referenced in building codes.

Minimum requirement: Homes must receive at least 0.35 air changes per hour (ACH) from outdoor air, with a minimum of 15 cubic feet per minute (CFM) per person. This baseline is intended to dilute odors and general indoor pollutants.

item.title: CARB Certification (Air Cleaners)
item.description:
CARB certification is a regulatory requirement for air cleaners sold in, shipped to, or used in California. It certifies that the device does not produce harmful ozone and meets electrical safety standards.

Regulatory Basis

California Assembly Bill 2276 (AB 2276, 2006, Pavley) mandated that CARB establish and enforce limits on ozone emissions from indoor air cleaning devices. The regulation went into effect in 2008 to protect public health from ozone pollution generated by air cleaning technologies.

item.title: Clean Air Delivery Rate (CADR)
item.description:
CADR is the volume of filtered air an air purifier delivers per minute for a specific pollutant type, measured under controlled laboratory conditions. It connects theoretical filter efficiency to real-world performance by accounting for both the filter’s removal capability and the device’s airflow rate.

Definition and Formula

CADR is calculated as the fraction of particles removed by the purifier (accounting for natural settling), multiplied by the room airflow rate in cubic feet per minute (CFM).

item.title: HEPA Classification
item.description:
HEPA filters are classified into performance tiers (H10–H14 in Europe; True HEPA in the US) based on their ability to remove particles at the most challenging size to filter. The classification depends on which standard is applied.

EN 1822 / ISO 29463 Standard

The European standard EN 1822 (now superseded by ISO 29463) establishes HEPA classification by measuring filtration efficiency at the Most Penetrating Particle Size (MPPS), which is approximately 0.21 micrometers. This size represents the diameter at which the filter is least efficient because both diffusion and interception mechanisms are comparatively ineffective.
